message.content:
- Using good benchmarks is important in performance evaluation theory
- Mutual funds often use mismatched benchmarks that do not capture their exposures to common factors in returns
- Some mutual fund investors may be unsophisticated and not pay attention to this, creating incentives for agents to strategically influence which benchmark is used
- Fund investors do pay attention to mismatched benchmarks when directing flows
- This behavior may be irrational and influenced by a behavioral element
- Mismatched self-designated benchmarks may reflect funds' strategic incentives to improve expected flows
- The evidence emphasizes the need for the development of measures of mutual fund performance that are both well grounded in economic theory and not subject to gaming.
